title: Remarks for the Panel: On the Safe-Haven Status of the Dollar
summary: Primarily a panel talk on the dollar’s safe-haven and reserve-currency status, but Hammack ties it to monetary policy and Fed independence. She notes US inflation is 'too high and has been above our objective for the past five years' with broad-based pressures (health insurance, electricity, tariffs), while also citing labor-market softening over the past year. With the funds rate around neutral after recent cuts, she judges policy 'in a good position' and expects to be on hold 'for quite some time,' seeing two-sided risks. She stresses central-bank independence and credibility as essential to anchoring 2 percent inflation and to the dollar’s status.

key_quotes.quote: Under my base case, I think policy should be on hold for quite some time as we see evidence that inflation is coming down and the labor market stabilizes further.
Currently, US inflation is too high and has been above our objective for the past five years.
Given this combination and recent rate reductions, I believe policy is in a good position. The fed funds rate is around neutral, which allows us to see how things are going to play out.
key_quotes.context: Her assessment that policy is near neutral, not clearly restrictive

key_quotes.quote: The Fed’s independence, along with its accountability to Congress, is critical to maintain the world’s confidence in our commitment to 2 percent inflation.
key_quotes.context: Emphasis on Fed independence and credibility
